faqs.q: Is antidepressant discontinuation syndrome the same as addiction?
faqs.a: No. Antidepressants don't cause craving or compulsive use the way addictive substances do. Discontinuation symptoms reflect the body adjusting to a change, not a drug dependency in the addictive sense.

faqs.q: How long does discontinuation syndrome last?
faqs.a: Symptoms usually start within a few days of stopping and ease over about one to two weeks. They include flu-like feelings, dizziness, nausea, headache, trouble sleeping, irritability, and brief electric-shock sensations sometimes called brain zaps.

faqs.q: How do you avoid discontinuation syndrome?
faqs.a: It's usually prevented by tapering the dose gradually with a prescriber rather than stopping suddenly. Never stop or change an antidepressant on your own, and if you've stopped abruptly and feel unwell, contact your prescriber.
